whyWrongByChoice.A: Galactomannan is specific for Aspergillus, not Pneumocystis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Procalcitonin typically rises in bacterial infection, not fungal pneumonia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryptococcal antigen detects Cryptococcus neoformans, not Pneumocystis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspergillus-specific IgG diagnoses chronic pulmonary aspergillosis, not PCP.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with longstanding asthma presents with nasal obstruction, facial pressure, and decreased olfaction. CT shows nasal polyposis with opacification of both maxillary sinuses and differential densities suggesting dense mucin within the sinuses. Which diagnosis is most likely?

explanation.summary: Nasal polyposis, sinus opacification, and hyperdense eosinophilic mucin in an atopic/asthmatic patient = AFRS.
explanation.whyCorrect: AFRS is a chronic allergic inflammation directed against colonizing fungi, typically in atopic patients. Hallmarks include nasal polyposis, sinus opacification with differential densities from eosinophilic mucin accumulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mucormycosis features acute necrosis, black eschar, and rapid angioinvasion, not chronic polyposis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute bacterial sinusitis is typically short-lived without polyposis or hyperdense mucin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IPA affects lung parenchyma, not the sinuses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GPA produces destructive granulomatous lesions, not eosinophilic polypoid sinusitis.

questions.stem: A clinician is comparing hyphal morphology of two mold pathogens. Organism 1 has broad, ribbon-like, non-septate hyphae with ~90 degree branching. Organism 2 has narrower, septate hyphae with ~45 degree branching. Which statement is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Organism 1 is Aspergillus; organism 2 is Mucor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Organism 1 is Candida; organism 2 is Cryptococcus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Organism 1 is Mucor; organism 2 is Aspergillus

explanation.summary: Broad non-septate hyphae with 90-degree branching = Mucor; septate hyphae with 45-degree branching = Aspergillus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mucorales produce broad ribbon-like non-septate hyphae with right-angle (90 degrees) branching. Aspergillus species produce narrower septate hyphae with acute-angle (~45 degrees) branching.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Reversed; broad non-septate right-angle is Mucorales.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspergillus hyphae are exclusively septate with acute-angle branching.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pneumocystis lives as trophozoites and cysts, not hyphae.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Candida forms pseudohyphae and Cryptococcus is a yeast; neither matches these morphologies.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with untreated HIV has a CD4 count of 40 cells/uL. He presents with headache, fever, and confusion. Lumbar puncture shows lymphocytic pleocytosis. India ink staining of CSF reveals encapsulated round organisms. Which organism is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: Encapsulated yeast on India ink in CSF of advanced HIV = Cryptococcus neoformans.
explanation.whyCorrect: India ink staining of CSF highlights the polysaccharide capsule of Cryptococcus neoformans as a clear halo around the yeast cell. Cryptococcal meningoencephalitis is common in advanced HIV with CD4 <100.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pneumocystis causes pneumonia, not encapsulated meningitis findings on India ink.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspergillus is a mold with septate hyphae, not an encapsulated yeast.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mucor is a non-septate mold, not a capsule-forming yeast.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Candida is a budding yeast with pseudohyphae but lacks a polysaccharide capsule demonstrable by India ink.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with asthma and ABPA is diagnosed and started on treatment. Which pairing best reflects first-line therapy with an adjunct aimed at reducing fungal burden?

explanation.summary: ABPA: systemic corticosteroids are first-line; antifungals (itraconazole/voriconazole) reduce fungal burden.
explanation.whyCorrect: ABPA is an immune-mediated disease. Corticosteroids address the inflammatory hypersensitivity reaction, and antifungals such as itraconazole or voriconazole are added to reduce airway fungal burden.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgery is used for aspergilloma, not ABPA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amphotericin B is not standard ABPA therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TMP-SMX/pentamidine treat Pneumocystis, not ABPA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Caspofungin/fluconazole are not used as first-line for ABPA.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes presents with fever, hemoptysis, and a rapidly progressive pulmonary infiltrate over 5 days. Biopsy shows broad non-septate hyphae invading pulmonary vessels. Which statement best explains the clinical course?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Angioinvasion causing thrombosis and tissue necrosis

explanation.summary: Pulmonary mucormycosis: angioinvasion -> thrombosis -> ischemic necrosis -> rapid progression with mortality 50-80%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mucorales are angioinvasive and penetrate tissue barriers rapidly, occluding blood vessels and causing ischemic necrosis. This explains fulminant progression with hemoptysis and very high mortality.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Slow colonization characterizes CPA/aspergilloma, not Mucorales.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IgE airway hyperreactivity describes ABPA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granulomatous/immune complex pathology describes hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mucorales are not primarily intracellular organisms.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with asthma has clinical and laboratory findings consistent with ABPA. Which laboratory test pattern best supports the diagnosis?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated Aspergillus IgE and IgG with eosinophilia

explanation.summary: ABPA: eosinophilia, elevated total serum IgE, and positive Aspergillus-specific IgE and IgG antibodies.
explanation.whyCorrect: ABPA is a Th2-driven hypersensitivity response. Classic labs include peripheral eosinophilia, markedly elevated total IgE, and positive Aspergillus-specific IgE and IgG antibodies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Galactomannan supports invasive aspergillosis, not ABPA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-D-glucan is not specific and does not establish ABPA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isolated Aspergillus IgG supports CPA/aspergilloma; ABPA also requires IgE and eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ABPA is defined by elevated IgE; IgM has no role.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with AML has suspected invasive aspergillosis. Bronchoalveolar lavage is sent for testing. Which combination most strongly supports a presumptive diagnosis when invasive tissue biopsy is not feasible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive BAL galactomannan plus BAL PCR for Aspergillus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive cryptococcal antigen plus India ink

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive beta-D-glucan plus silver stain cysts

choices.label: E
choices.text: Positive acid-fast bacilli plus interferon-gamma release

explanation.summary: BAL galactomannan plus BAL PCR provides presumptive diagnosis of invasive aspergillosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the right clinical context, a positive BAL galactomannan, BAL PCR for Aspergillus, or positive serum galactomannan can establish a presumptive diagnosis of invasive aspergillosis when biopsy is not possible.

whyWrongByChoice.B: IgE and eosinophilia support ABPA, not invasive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryptococcal antigen and India ink diagnose cryptococcosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-D-glucan plus silver cysts supports PCP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AFB and IGRA diagnose tuberculosis, not aspergillosis.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man develops severe PCP while on high-dose glucocorticoids for polymyositis. He has a documented severe sulfa allergy with prior Stevens-Johnson syndrome. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative therapy?

explanation.summary: PCP second-line therapy for sulfa-allergic patients includes pentamidine, atovaquone, or clindamycin + primaquine.
explanation.whyCorrect: TMP-SMX is first-line for PCP. In patients with severe sulfa allergy, alternatives include pentamidine, atovaquone, or clindamycin plus primaquine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Voriconazole treats Aspergillus, not Pneumocystis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amphotericin B has no role in PCP treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Caspofungin has limited activity against Pneumocystis and is not standard second-line therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Itraconazole treats CPA/ABPA, not PCP.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old spelunker in Ohio develops fever, cough, and malaise 10 days after exploring a cave heavily populated by bats. Chest CT shows nodular pneumonia with mediastinal lymphadenopathy resembling miliary TB. Lung biopsy reveals caseating granulomas with small ovoid yeast inside macrophages exhibiting narrow-based budding. Which organism is responsible?

explanation.summary: Histoplasma: caves/bat droppings, intracellular yeast in macrophages, narrow-based budding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ohio River valley, bat exposure, and small intracellular yeast with narrow-based budding are pathognomonic for Histoplasma capsulatum.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blastomyces shows broad-based budding and is not intracellular.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coccidioides forms spherules, not intracellular yeast.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryptococcus is a large encapsulated yeast, not intracellular.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paracoccidioides is endemic to Latin America with captain's wheel morphology.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old forestry worker from Wisconsin develops subacute pneumonia with productive cough and a verrucous skin lesion on his forearm. Sputum KOH prep reveals large, thick-walled yeast with broad-based budding. Which is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

explanation.summary: Mild-moderate blastomycosis: itraconazole first-line.
explanation.whyCorrect: Broad-based budding yeast from the Great Lakes region with mild-to-moderate disease is blastomycosis; itraconazole is first-line.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amphotericin B is reserved for severe/disseminated disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fluconazole is second-line for blastomycosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Voriconazole is not first-line for blastomycosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flucytosine is adjunctive for cryptococcal infection, not blastomycosis.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old construction worker in Arizona's Central Valley develops fever, dry cough, pleuritic chest pain, and painful red nodules on his shins 2 weeks after working in dusty soil. Biopsy of a pulmonary nodule shows large structures filled with small endospores. Which is the most likely organism?

explanation.summary: Valley fever with spherules and erythema nodosum indicates Coccidioides.
explanation.whyCorrect: SW US desert dust exposure with spherule-endospore morphology and erythema nodosum is classic for Coccidioides.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Histoplasma does not form spherules; associated with bat/bird droppings.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Blastomyces forms broad-based budding yeast, not spherules.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryptococcus gattii is an encapsulated yeast in the Pacific Northwest.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paracoccidioides is in Latin America and forms captain's wheel.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old Brazilian agricultural worker who recently immigrated to the US presents with 2 months of productive cough, weight loss, and painful ulcerative lesions on the oral mucosa. Biopsy shows large yeast with multiple narrow-based buds arranged circumferentially around a central mother cell. Which organism is responsible?

explanation.summary: Captain's wheel yeast with oral mucosal lesions in a Latin American patient = Paracoccidioides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Paracoccidioides is endemic to Latin America, commonly involves oral mucosa, and has the pathognomonic captain's/mariner's wheel morphology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blastomyces shows single broad-based budding, not multiple buds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Histoplasma is small intracellular yeast with single narrow-based budding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coccidioides forms spherules, not budding yeast.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cryptococcus is a single encapsulated yeast without multiple buds.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with AIDS (CD4 25) presents with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia. He lived in Kentucky as a child. Bone marrow biopsy shows diffuse small intracellular organisms with minimal granuloma formation. Which factor best explains the poorly formed granulomas?

explanation.summary: Granuloma formation requires Th1/IFN-gamma-activated macrophages, impaired in AIDS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Effective granulomas need a strong Th1 cell-mediated response with IFN-gamma activation of macrophages; AIDS impairs this, producing diffuse organisms with minimal granulomatous inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Histoplasma lacks a prominent capsule.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neutrophil burst defects relate to chronic granulomatous disease, not AIDS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Complement deficiency is not the cause of granuloma failure in AIDS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: B-cell/antibody function is largely preserved in AIDS; T-cell loss is the issue.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old smoker with COPD in Tennessee has 6 months of progressive cough, weight loss, and cavitary upper lobe lesions on CT. Sputum GMS stain reveals small intracellular yeast with narrow-based budding. Which complicating diagnosis should be most strongly considered?

explanation.summary: Chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis in smokers: consider cancer on differential.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF specifically notes that cancer should be considered because smokers are at risk for both chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis and lung cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspergillus is possible but not specifically highlighted in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.B: TB is on the differential but cancer is the specific concern noted for smokers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sarcoidosis does not fit the smoker/cavitary picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PE does not produce cavitary upper lobe lesions.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with HIV/AIDS in Oklahoma presents with headache, fever, and altered mental status. LP shows elevated opening pressure and lymphocytic pleocytosis. India ink stain shows large yeast with a prominent capsule. Which test is most sensitive for diagnosis?

explanation.summary: CrAg is the most sensitive test for Cryptococcus.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) test in serum or CSF is very sensitive and has largely replaced India ink for rapid diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Galactomannan detects Histoplasma/Aspergillus, not Cryptococcus.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-D-glucan is nonspecific and Cryptococcus has low levels.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunodiffusion is used for Coccidioides.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chocolate agar is for Haemophilus, not fungi.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old AIDS patient has severe disseminated cryptococcal meningitis. Which regimen is most appropriate for initial induction therapy?

explanation.summary: Severe Cryptococcus: amphotericin B + flucytosine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the table, severe/disseminated cryptococcal disease requires amphotericin B; flucytosine is added specifically for Cryptococcus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Itraconazole is not first-line for severe cryptococcal disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fluconazole alone is maintenance therapy, not induction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Voriconazole/caspofungin are not the standard cryptococcal induction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Posaconazole is not standard induction therapy.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man has suspected fungal pneumonia. Urine galactomannan antigen returns positive. Which pair of organisms can this assay NOT distinguish between?

explanation.summary: Galactomannan is shared by Histoplasma and Aspergillus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both Histoplasma and Aspergillus possess galactomannan antigen, so the assay cannot distinguish them. Candida, Blastomyces, Coccidioides, and Cryptococcus do not have galactomannan.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Neither Blastomyces nor Coccidioides has galactomannan.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neither Cryptococcus nor Candida has galactomannan.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryptococcus lacks galactomannan.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Candida lacks galactomannan.

questions.stem: A sputum specimen is cultured on Sabouraud agar at 25°C and grows septate hyphae forming barrel-shaped arthroconidia. At 37°C, tissue biopsy shows large sac-like structures filled with small endospores rather than budding yeast. Which geographic exposure fits best?

explanation.summary: Arthroconidia and spherules = Coccidioides = SW US desert.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coccidioides forms arthroconidia in mold phase and spherules filled with endospores in tissue; endemic to arid SW US including Arizona.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mississippi caves suggest Histoplasma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Great Lakes forests suggest Blastomyces.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pacific NW decaying wood suggests C. gattii.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Brazil suggests Paracoccidioides.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man in Washington state develops cough and mental status changes. MRI shows multiple pulmonary and cerebral nodular lesions (cryptococcomas). He has no immunocompromise. Which organism is most consistent with this presentation?

explanation.summary: Pacific Northwest + immunocompetent host + cryptococcomas = C. gattii.
explanation.whyCorrect: C. gattii is predominantly found in the Pacific Northwest and notably infects immunocompetent hosts, producing cryptococcomas.

whyWrongByChoice.A: C. neoformans is nationwide and typically affects immunocompromised patients (pigeon droppings).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blastomyces is endemic to the east, not PNW.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Histoplasma is endemic to Ohio/Mississippi valleys.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coccidioides is in SW desert regions.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected fungal pneumonia has a urine antigen test positive for Blastomyces. The lab cautions that this result may not be definitive. Which organism most commonly cross-reacts with this assay?

explanation.summary: Blastomyces urine antigen cross-reacts with Histoplasma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF explicitly notes cross-reactivity of the Blastomyces urine antigen test with Histoplasma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Coccidioides is detected by serum/CSF immunodiffusion without this cross-reactivity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryptococcus is detected by CrAg without cross-reactivity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspergillus shares galactomannan with Histoplasma, not Blastomyces.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Candida does not share this cross-reactivity.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man who lived in Ohio 20 years ago and now lives in New York begins chemotherapy for lymphoma. He develops fever, pancytopenia, and hepatosplenomegaly. Urine antigen is positive for Histoplasma. Which best explains his infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recent acute exposure in New York

explanation.summary: Histoplasma establishes latency; reactivation occurs with immunosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Like TB, Histoplasma can persist in macrophages for decades and reactivate when immunosuppression develops (e.g., chemotherapy).

whyWrongByChoice.A: NY is not endemic for Histoplasma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Contamination does not explain the clinical syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cross-reactivity doesn't explain clinical disseminated disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chemotherapy immunosuppression is the inciting factor, not congenital immunodeficiency.

questions.stem: A mold form of a dimorphic fungus shows septate hyphae bearing BOTH microconidia and large tuberculated sun-shaped macroconidia on Sabouraud agar at 25°C. Which organism is this?

explanation.summary: Sun-shaped macroconidia are characteristic of Histoplasma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Histoplasma mold has both microconidia and large sun-shaped (tuberculated) macroconidia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blastomyces mold has only microconidia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coccidioides mold produces arthroconidia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryptococcus is not thermally dimorphic and has no mold form.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paracoccidioides mold does not show these sun-shaped macroconidia.

questions.stem: A patient with pulmonary disease has a fungal culture that does NOT exhibit thermal dimorphism on Sabouraud agar at 25°C vs 37°C. Which organism is most consistent?

explanation.summary: Cryptococcus is a yeast only, not thermally dimorphic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cryptococcus spp. are considered yeasts without a clear mold form and are not thermally dimorphic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Histoplasma is thermally dimorphic.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Blastomyces is thermally dimorphic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coccidioides is dimorphic (mold with arthroconidia; spherules in tissue).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paracoccidioides is thermally dimorphic.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old poultry farmer in Arkansas has mild self-limited pulmonary histoplasmosis with persistent mild symptoms for 3 weeks. He remains ambulatory and is otherwise healthy. Which is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

explanation.summary: Mild-moderate histoplasmosis with persistent symptoms: itraconazole.
explanation.whyCorrect: Itraconazole is first-line for mild-to-moderate histoplasmosis; it is used for persistent symptoms or disseminated disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Persistent symptoms warrant treatment rather than observation only.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amphotericin B is reserved for severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Voriconazole is not first-line for histoplasmosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flucytosine is adjunctive for Cryptococcus.

questions.stem: A patient suspected of fungal CNS disease undergoes CSF antigen testing. Which assay is the recommended CSF test for identifying Coccidioides infection?

explanation.summary: Coccidioides: serum and CSF immunodiffusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the PDF, Coccidioides is identified via serum and CSF immunodiffusion assay.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Galactomannan is for Histoplasma/Aspergillus.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-D-glucan is not the specified test for Coccidioides.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CrAg is for Cryptococcus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Urine antigen is for Histoplasma (and Blastomyces).

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man from Minnesota presents with subacute pneumonia and a verrucous cutaneous lesion with irregular borders on his forearm. Which is the most common site of dissemination for this organism?

explanation.summary: Blastomyces: skin is the most common site of dissemination.
explanation.whyCorrect: Skin involvement (verrucous lesions) is the most common extrapulmonary site for Blastomyces dermatitidis (~18%).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lymph nodes are more classic for disseminated histoplasmosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CNS involvement is uncommon (~1%) for Blastomyces.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bone marrow dissemination is more typical of disseminated histoplasmosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral mucosal lesions are characteristic of Paracoccidioides.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with HIV (CD4 40) in California develops meningitis but has no fever or nuchal rigidity, only altered mental status. Which factor best explains the atypical presentation?

explanation.summary: Severely immunocompromised patients may lack classic inflammatory signs.
explanation.whyCorrect: In advanced AIDS, the inflammatory response is impaired so classic meningeal signs (fever, nuchal rigidity) may be absent; special attention is warranted to neurologic symptoms alone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No antibiotic pretreatment is described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AIDS patients often have very high fungal burdens.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Altered mental status is not an early feature alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Host immune detection is intrinsically impaired, not evaded.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with mild Valley fever (fever, dry cough) has a positive Coccidioides immunodiffusion and is otherwise healthy without dissemination. Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

explanation.summary: Mild-moderate coccidioidomycosis: fluconazole first-line.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the treatment table, fluconazole is first-line for mild to moderate coccidioidomycosis; itraconazole is second-line.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Itraconazole is second-line for coccidioidomycosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amphotericin B is reserved for severe/disseminated disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Voriconazole is not first-line.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Echinocandins are not effective against Coccidioides.

questions.stem: A patient with Crohn's disease develops painful red nodular lesions on her shins. She has no travel history or fungal exposure. Which best describes the likely pathophysiology of the skin lesions?

explanation.summary: Erythema nodosum can arise from non-infectious causes like Crohn's, representing an immune reaction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythema nodosum is an immune-mediated reaction presenting as painful red nodules on the shins; it can occur from fungal antigens or non-infectious causes such as Crohn's disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No fungal exposure; this is not dissemination.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cellulitis is diffuse and unilateral, not bilateral shin nodules.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No infectious granuloma is present.
whyWrongByChoice.E: EN is septal panniculitis, not vasculitis with organism invasion.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old landscaper in Indiana develops severe disseminated blastomycosis with hypoxic respiratory failure and skin lesions. Which is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

explanation.summary: Severe/disseminated blastomycosis: amphotericin B initial therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the treatment table, severe or disseminated blastomycosis (as well as histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, and cryptococcosis) is treated with amphotericin B as initial therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Itraconazole is first-line only for mild-moderate disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fluconazole is used for maintenance after amphotericin B.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Voriconazole is not standard for severe blastomycosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Posaconazole is not standard initial therapy.
